mnemonics.title: ABSTRACTION - Always Bring Simple Things Ready And Comfortable To Hide Internal Operations
mnemonics.content:
A - Always
B - Bring
S - Simple
T - Things
R - Ready
A - And
C - Comfortable
T - To
I - Hide
O - Internal
N - Operations
This reminds that abstraction hides internal complexity and shows only what's necessary.

mnemonics.title: ENCAPSULATION - Every New Code Should Protect Users' Secret Data Inside
mnemonics.content:
E - Every
N - New
C - Code
S - Should
P - Protect
U - Users'
L - Data
A - And
T - Totally
I - Isolate
O - Objects
N - New
This emphasizes data protection and bundling within classes.

mnemonics.title: ABC - Abstract Base Classes
mnemonics.content: In Python, use 'from abc import ABC, abstractmethod' to create abstract classes that define blueprints for other classes.

mnemonics.title: Getter-Setter Gold Rule
mnemonics.content: Get what you need, Set what you change, but keep data Private - GESSP: Get External, Set Smartly, Stay Private!

acronyms.usage: Use when you want to create a blueprint class that cannot be instantiated directly. Use @abstractmethod decorator to define methods that subclasses must implement.
Use _variable_name to indicate a protected attribute. It's a convention telling developers 'don't access directly from outside' but Python doesn't enforce it.
Use __variable_name for stronger privacy. Python performs name mangling (_ClassName__variable) making it harder to access from outside the class.
Use @property to create getter methods that allow controlled access to private attributes, making them appear like regular attributes.
One of SOLID principles - clients should not be forced to depend on interfaces they do not use. Related to abstraction in OOP.

visualTips.visualization: Picture an ATM. You see buttons for 'Withdraw', 'Balance', 'Pin'. Inside, there's complex circuitry, security protocols, database connections. You interact with simple buttons (interface) without knowing what's happening inside (implementation hidden).

Think of Russian nesting dolls. Each doll (class) contains smaller dolls (data/methods) inside it. The outer surface protects the inner contents - you can only see what's exposed, the rest is sealed inside.

A remote control has buttons (abstract interface). You press 'Volume +' and sound increases. You don't see the electronic signals, speaker vibrations, or circuit processing. The complexity is hidden, only the function is visible.

Imagine a safe with a lock. The valuables (data) are inside. To access them, you need a key or code (getter/setter methods). The safe walls (class boundaries) protect what's inside from unauthorized access.

Think of an architect's blueprint. It shows where rooms, doors, windows will be (method signatures) but doesn't build the actual house. The blueprint is abstract - it defines WHAT but not HOW. Subclasses 'build' the actual implementation.

analogies.analogy: Driving a car. You use steering wheel, accelerator, brake (interface). You don't need to know about engine combustion, transmission gears, or brake hydraulics (implementation details). The car's complexity is abstracted away - you just 'drive'.

A restaurant kitchen. The menu (public interface) shows available dishes. The kitchen (class) contains recipes, ingredients, cooking equipment (data and methods). Customers can't enter the kitchen - they only interact through the menu. The cooking process is encapsulated within the kitchen.

A vehicle specification document. It says 'all vehicles must have wheels, engine, brakes' (abstract methods). But the document itself can't drive (can't be instantiated). Car, Bike, Truck classes inherit from this and provide actual implementations.

A hotel room with a safe. Your valuables (sensitive data) are inside the safe (private attribute). You need a PIN code (getter/setter) to access or modify. Other hotel guests (external code) cannot just take your things - they're protected.

A smartphone charger. The USB end (interface) is the same across all chargers. But the plug type and internal circuitry (implementation) differs. You plug in (use abstraction) without worrying about voltage differences (implementation hidden).
